markdownRemark.html:
A review of the Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models paper published in 2020.

DDPM is one of the most important papers that drove the transition of the generative modeling field from GAN-centric research to diffusion-based approaches prior to 2020.

Most generative models work by sampling random noise and transforming it into high-quality samples, and diffusion models are no different. As shown in the image above, noise is sequentially injected into the data denoted as x0x_0x0​ to transform it into N(0,I)\mathcal N(0, I)N(0,I), and the model then learns the reverse process. This way, the model can ultimately generate high-quality samples from random noise sampled from N(0,I)\mathcal N(0, I)N(0,I).

Background

In diffusion models, there are concepts called the forward process (diffusion process) qqq and the backward process pθp_\thetapθ​, each expressed by the following equations.

  • x1:Tx_{1:T}x1:T​: Intermediate states where noise is progressively added at each step from x0x_0x0​ to xTx_TxT​
  • pθ(xT)=N(xT;0,I)p_\theta(x_{T}) = \mathcal N (x_T;0, I)pθ​(xT​)=N(xT​;0,I) is defined, and pθ(x0:T)p_\theta(x_{0:T})pθ​(x0:T​) is defined as a Markov chain
  • x1,⋯ ,xTx_1, \cdots, x_Tx1​,⋯,xT​ are latent variables that exist in the same dimensional pixel space as x0x_0x0​

Here, the goal of pθp_\thetapθ​ (the model) is to predict what μ\muμ and Σ\SigmaΣ the distribution of xt−1x_{t-1}xt−1​ should have given xtx_txt​. Therefore, the conditional probability pθp_\thetapθ​ can be expressed as follows:

pθ(xt−1∣xt):=N(xt−1;μθ(xt,t),Σθ(xt,t))p_\theta\left(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{x}_t\right):=\mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} ; \boldsymbol{\mu}_\theta\left(\mathbf{x}_t, t\right), \mathbf{\Sigma}_\theta\left(\mathbf{x}_t, t\right)\right)pθ​(xt−1​∣xt​):=N(xt−1​;μθ​(xt​,t),Σθ​(xt​,t))

Moving on to the model training phase, since all generative models aim to maximize the likelihood of pθ(x0)p_\theta(x_0)pθ​(x0​), the loss can be set up to minimize −log⁡pθ(x0)-\log p_\theta(x_0)−logpθ​(x0​), which is expressed mathematically as follows. The detailed derivation can be found in Appendix A of the paper.

E[−log⁡pθ(x0)]≤Eq[−log⁡pθ(x0:T)q(x1:T∣x0)]=Eq[−log⁡p(xT)−∑t≥1log⁡pθ(xt−1∣xt)q(xt∣xt−1)]=:L\mathbb{E}\left[-\log p_\theta\left(\mathbf{x}_0\right)\right] \leq \mathbb{E}_q\left[-\log \frac{p_\theta\left(\mathbf{x}_{0: T}\right)}{q\left(\mathbf{x}_{1: T} \mid \mathbf{x}_0\right)}\right]=\mathbb{E}_q\left[-\log p\left(\mathbf{x}_T\right)-\sum_{t \geq 1} \log \frac{p_\theta\left(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{x}_t\right)}{q\left(\mathbf{x}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_{t-1}\right)}\right]=: LE[−logpθ​(x0​)]≤Eq​[−logq(x1:T​∣x0​)pθ​(x0:T​)​]=Eq​[−logp(xT​)−t≥1∑​logq(xt​∣xt−1​)pθ​(xt−1​∣xt​)​]=:L
Eq[DKL(q(xT∣x0)∥p(xT))⏟LT+∑t>1DKL(q(xt−1∣xt,x0)∥pθ(xt−1∣xt))⏟Lt−1−log⁡pθ(x0∣x1)⏟L0]\mathbb{E}_q[\underbrace{D_{\mathrm{KL}}\left(q\left(\mathbf{x}_T \mid \mathbf{x}_0\right) \| p\left(\mathbf{x}_T\right)\right)}_{L_T}+\sum_{t>1} \underbrace{D_{\mathrm{KL}}\left(q\left(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0\right) \| p_\theta\left(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{x}_t\right)\right)}_{L_{t-1}} \underbrace{-\log p_\theta\left(\mathbf{x}_0 \mid \mathbf{x}_1\right)}_{L_0}]Eq​[LT​DKL​(q(xT​∣x0​)∥p(xT​))​​+t>1∑​Lt−1​DKL​(q(xt−1​∣xt​,x0​)∥pθ​(xt−1​∣xt​))​​L0​−logpθ​(x0​∣x1​)​​]

In the loss equation above, we need to look more closely at Lt−1L_{t-1}Lt−1​, which corresponds to the diffusion model. Here, q(xt−1∣xt,x0)q(x_{t-1} | x_t, x_0)q(xt−1​∣xt​,x0​), which pθ(xt−1∣xt)p_\theta(x_{t-1}|x_t)pθ​(xt−1​∣xt​) should resemble, is expressed as follows. Here, βt\beta_tβt​ denotes the variance of the data at timestep ttt in the forward process.

q(xt−1∣xt,x0)=N(xt−1;μ~t(xt,x0),β~tI), where μ~t(xt,x0):=αˉt−1βt1−αˉtx0+αt(1−αˉt−1)1−αˉtxt and β~t:=1−αˉt−11−αˉtβt\begin{aligned} q\left(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0\right) & =\mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} ; \tilde{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t\left(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0\right), \tilde{\beta}_t \mathbf{I}\right), \\ \text { where } \quad \tilde{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t\left(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0\right) & :=\frac{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} \beta_t}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t} \mathbf{x}_0+\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_t}\left(1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}\right)}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t} \mathbf{x}_t \quad \text { and } \quad \tilde{\beta}_t:=\frac{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t} \beta_t \end{aligned}q(xt−1​∣xt​,x0​) where μ~​t​(xt​,x0​)​=N(xt−1​;μ~​t​(xt​,x0​),β~​t​I),:=1−αˉt​αˉt−1​​βt​​x0​+1−αˉt​αt​​(1−αˉt−1​)​xt​ and β~​t​:=1−αˉt​1−αˉt−1​​βt​​

Once you accept that re-expressing q(xt∣xt−1)q(x_{t} | x_{t-1})q(xt​∣xt−1​) in the form of q(xt−1∣xt,x0)q(x_{t-1} | x_t, x_0)q(xt−1​∣xt​,x0​) yields this result, the Background section is complete.

Diffusion Models and Denoising Autoencoders

The DDPM authors revisit and reorganize the basic loss equation of diffusion models term by term.

Eq[DKL(q(xT∣x0)∥p(xT))⏟LT+∑t>1DKL(q(xt−1∣xt,x0)∥pθ(xt−1∣xt))⏟Lt−1−log⁡pθ(x0∣x1)⏟L0]\mathbb{E}_q[\underbrace{D_{\mathrm{KL}}\left(q\left(\mathbf{x}_T \mid \mathbf{x}_0\right) \| p\left(\mathbf{x}_T\right)\right)}_{L_T}+\sum_{t>1} \underbrace{D_{\mathrm{KL}}\left(q\left(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0\right) \| p_\theta\left(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{x}_t\right)\right)}_{L_{t-1}} \underbrace{-\log p_\theta\left(\mathbf{x}_0 \mid \mathbf{x}_1\right)}_{L_0}]Eq​[LT​DKL​(q(xT​∣x0​)∥p(xT​))​​+t>1∑​Lt−1​DKL​(q(xt−1​∣xt​,x0​)∥pθ​(xt−1​∣xt​))​​L0​−logpθ​(x0​∣x1​)​​]

Let us first examine the LTL_TLT​ term. In the equation, p(xT)p(x_T)p(xT​) is fixed as N(xT;0,I)\mathcal N(x_T; 0, I)N(xT​;0,I), and q(xt∣x0)q(x_t|x_0)q(xt​∣x0​) varies depending on βt\beta_tβt​. In this paper, βt\beta_tβt​ is set as a constant, so LTL_TLT​ becomes irrelevant to parameter optimization and can be ignored.

Next, let us examine the Lt−1L_{t-1}Lt−1​ term. Writing the distributions q(xt−1∣xt,x0)q(x_{t-1}|x_t, x_0)q(xt−1​∣xt​,x0​) and pθ(xt−1∣xt)p_\theta(x_{t-1}|x_t)pθ​(xt−1​∣xt​) as described in the Background section above:

q(xt−1∣xt,x0)=N(xt−1;μ~t(xt,x0),β~tI), where μ~t(xt,x0):=αˉt−1βt1−αˉtx0+αt(1−αˉt−1)1−αˉtxt and β~t:=1−αˉt−11−αˉtβt\begin{aligned} q\left(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0\right) & =\mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} ; \tilde{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t\left(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0\right), \tilde{\beta}_t \mathbf{I}\right), \\ \text { where } \quad \tilde{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t\left(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0\right) & :=\frac{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}} \beta_t}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t} \mathbf{x}_0+\frac{\sqrt{\alpha_t}\left(1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}\right)}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t} \mathbf{x}_t \quad \text { and } \quad \tilde{\beta}_t:=\frac{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}{1-\bar{\alpha}_t} \beta_t \end{aligned}q(xt−1​∣xt​,x0​) where μ~​t​(xt​,x0​)​=N(xt−1​;μ~​t​(xt​,x0​),β~​t​I),:=1−αˉt​αˉt−1​​βt​​x0​+1−αˉt​αt​​(1−αˉt−1​)​xt​ and β~​t​:=1−αˉt​1−αˉt−1​​βt​​
pθ(xt−1∣xt):=N(xt−1;μθ(xt,t),Σθ(xt,t))p_\theta\left(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} \mid \mathbf{x}_t\right):=\mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{x}_{t-1} ; \boldsymbol{\mu}_\theta\left(\mathbf{x}_t, t\right), \mathbf{\Sigma}_\theta\left(\mathbf{x}_t, t\right)\right)pθ​(xt−1​∣xt​):=N(xt−1​;μθ​(xt​,t),Σθ​(xt​,t))

The parameters we need to learn are ultimately μθ\mu_\thetaμθ​ and Σθ\Sigma_\thetaΣθ​, which predict the mean and covariance of q(xt−1∣xt,x0)q(x_{t-1}|x_t, x_0)q(xt−1​∣xt​,x0​) at timestep ttt. However, in the paper, Σθ\Sigma_\thetaΣθ​ is set as a time-dependent constant of the form σt2I\sigma_t^2Iσt2​I. This means the only parameter Lt−1L_{t-1}Lt−1​ needs to learn is μθ\mu_\thetaμθ​. Therefore, rearranging this in L2 loss form yields:

Lt−1=Eq[12σt2∥μ~t(xt,x0)−μθ(xt,t)∥2]+CL_{t-1}=\mathbb{E}_q\left[\frac{1}{2 \sigma_t^2}\left\|\tilde{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_t\left(\mathbf{x}_t, \mathbf{x}_0\right)-\boldsymbol{\mu}_\theta\left(\mathbf{x}_t, t\right)\right\|^2\right]+CLt−1​=Eq​[2σt2​1​∥μ~​t​(xt​,x0​)−μθ​(xt​,t)∥2]+C

By further simplifying this equation, it can be reduced to the following form:

Ex0,ϵ[βt22σt2αt(1−αˉt)∥ϵ−ϵθ(αˉtx0+1−αˉtϵ,t)∥2]\mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}_0, \boldsymbol{\epsilon}}\left[\frac{\beta_t^2}{2 \sigma_t^2 \alpha_t\left(1-\bar{\alpha}_t\right)}\left\|\boldsymbol{\epsilon}-\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta\left(\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} \mathbf{x}_0+\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t} \boldsymbol{\epsilon}, t\right)\right\|^2\right]Ex0​,ϵ​[2σt2​αt​(1−αˉt​)βt2​​​ϵ−ϵθ​(αˉt​​x0​+1−αˉt​​ϵ,t)​2]

A key point to note in this simplification process is that 'the problem has shifted from predicting the mean μ~\tilde\muμ~​ to predicting the noise ϵ\epsilonϵ.' Once the equation has been simplified to this point, training and sampling become possible.

The L0L_0L0​ term is similar to the Lt−1(t>1)L_{t-1} (t>1)Lt−1​(t>1) equation with t=1t=1t=1 substituted, so it is merged into the Lt−1L_{t-1}Lt−1​ term. By applying equal weighting across all timesteps in the original Lt−1L_{t-1}Lt−1​ term, the following simplified loss is obtained:

Lsimple (θ):=Et,x0,ϵ[∥ϵ−ϵθ(αˉtx0+1−αˉtϵ,t)∥2]L_{\text {simple }}(\theta):=\mathbb{E}_{t, \mathbf{x}_0, \boldsymbol{\epsilon}}\left[\left\|\boldsymbol{\epsilon}-\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta\left(\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} \mathbf{x}_0+\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t} \boldsymbol{\epsilon}, t\right)\right\|^2\right]Lsimple ​(θ):=Et,x0​,ϵ​[​ϵ−ϵθ​(αˉt​​x0​+1−αˉt​​ϵ,t)​2]

Experiments

For the experiments, T=1000T=1000T=1000 was set, and βt\beta_tβt​ was configured to increase linearly from β1=10−4\beta_1=10^{-4}β1​=10−4 to βT=0.02\beta_T=0.02βT​=0.02. The backbone for the reverse process uses a U-Net, and the same U-Net model with shared parameters is used across all timesteps.

Quantitative results were evaluated using Inception Score (IS) and FID.

It is also possible to apply the forward process to two original images, then restore linearly interpolated noise between them.

Reference

Ho, Jonathan, Ajay Jain, and Pieter Abbeel. "Denoising diffusion probabilistic models." Advances in neural information processing systems 33 (2020): 6840-6851.
